coredata.dc:title: Lophatherum-Gracile driven near-infrared carbon dots inhibit ferroptosis and mitophagy to treat acute kidney injury

coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) based-biomass are known for their excellent safety but often face challenges such as short emission (Em) wavelength and low quantum yield (QY). Here, using the traditional Chinese medicinal lophatherum gracile as a carbon source, we combined conventional decoction techniques with CDs preparation methods to obtain safe and green CDs (Lophatherum-Gracile driven near-infrared carbon dots, L-CDs) for the first time with an Em peak at 666 nm, a shoulder peak at 730 nm, and a QY of 13.36 %. L-CDs showed remarkable stability, safety, and resistance to photobleaching. Notably, it effectively quenched reactive oxygen species (ROS) and exhibited specific sensitivity to Fe(II). Additionally, L-CDs exhibited potential in treating ferroptosis-related inflammation and inhibiting mitophagy, and they were found to be effective in preventing and treating cisplatin (CDDP)-induced acute kidney injury (AKI). These effects were comparable to those of commercially available ferroptosis therapies-Ferrostatin-1 (Fer-1) and deferoxamine (DFO). Overall, our work presents novel, safe, and eco-friendly near-infrared L-CDs that can be effectively utilized for identifying cellular organelle and in vivo imaging, as well as for treating ferroptosis, mitochondrial-related inflammation, and chemotherapy-induced AKI. These findings broaden the potential applications of CDs in biomedical diagnosis and therapy, intending to advance clinical development.

head.ce:title: Lophatherum-Gracile driven near-infrared carbon dots inhibit ferroptosis and mitophagy to treat acute kidney injury

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) based-biomass are known for their excellent safety but often face challenges such as short emission (Em) wavelength and low quantum yield (QY). Here, using the traditional Chinese medicinal lophatherum gracile as a carbon source, we combined conventional decoction techniques with CDs preparation methods to obtain safe and green CDs (Lophatherum-Gracile driven near-infrared carbon dots, L-CDs) for the first time with an Em peak at 666 nm, a shoulder peak at 730 nm, and a QY of 13.36 %. L-CDs showed remarkable stability, safety, and resistance to photobleaching. Notably, it effectively quenched reactive oxygen species (ROS) and exhibited specific sensitivity to Fe(II). Additionally, L-CDs exhibited potential in treating ferroptosis-related inflammation and inhibiting mitophagy, and they were found to be effective in preventing and treating cisplatin (CDDP)-induced acute kidney injury (AKI). These effects were comparable to those of commercially available ferroptosis therapies-Ferrostatin-1 (Fer-1) and deferoxamine (DFO). Overall, our work presents novel, safe, and eco-friendly near-infrared L-CDs that can be effectively utilized for identifying cellular organelle and in vivo imaging, as well as for treating ferroptosis, mitochondrial-related inflammation, and chemotherapy-induced AKI. These findings broaden the potential applications of CDs in biomedical diagnosis and therapy, intending to advance clinical development.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Acute Kidney Injury (AKI) is marked by a swift reduction in kidney function and has seen an increasing incidence in recent years. Various factors can trigger AKI, as the chemotherapy agent cisplatin (CDDP), widely used for treating a range of cancers, and is a well-known inducer of AKI [1]. It can cause DNA damage, cellular redox imbalance, and mitochondrial dysfunction due to its reabsorption from the glomerular filtrate in the proximal renal tubules, further inducing ferroptosis and mitophagy that ultimately trigger the onset of AKI [2,3]. Ferroptosis is a key factor in AKI pathogenesis, driving oxidative stress and injury to kidney cells. Research indicates that modulating iron metabolism and blocking ferroptosis could be promising therapeutic approaches for AKI, with ferroptosis inhibitors already explored in AKI treatment, however, they are still in early stages of development. Presently, the main inhibitors include iron chelators like deferoxamine and deferasirox, as well as free radical scavengers/ antioxidants such as Vitamin E (α-Tocopherol), liproxstatin-1 (Lip-1) and Fer-1, and others, including natural products or small molecules with phenolic hydroxyl groups and regulatory proteins, like curcumin, baicalein, resveratrol, and sulforaphane [4]. However, despite these advancements, there are currently no ferroptosis inhibitors being utilized in clinical practice. This may be attributed to issues related to poor water solubility, short blood half-life [5], low efficacy, poor selectivity, high toxicity, and drug resistance, etc. [4]. To address these challenges, nanomedicine serves as a promising solution, as it offers excellent stability, suitable for targeted drug delivery, controlled release, and the potential to mitigate drug resistance [6]. Therefore, designing nanomedicine that can target kidney and mitochondria, scavenging reactive oxygen species (ROS), and simultaneously chelating Fe(II) is of great significance for AKI.

Carbon dots (CDs) have gained attention as a promising nano-fluorescent material, valued for their chemical stability, excellent thermal and optical properties, high water solubility, and outstanding biocompatibility [7]. Their small size enables easy penetration of cell membranes, which also facilitates hepatic and renal metabolism, allowing for passive targeting of the kidneys and showing promise for the treatment of kidney-related diseases [8,9]. The carbon source for producing CDs includes carbon-based materials, chemical small molecules, and/or natural products. Natural products offer certain advantages in the production of biomass for CDs, including eco-friendly and cost-effective processes, abundant raw materials, renewability, and large-scale synthesis [10]. Numerous experiments have demonstrated the high safety of biogenic CDs, emphasizing their considerable potential for biomedical diagnosis and therapy [11–13]. These advantages have laid a great foundation for the application of CDs as fluorescent nanomaterials in the treatment of renal inflammation and bioimaging. Unfortunately, most biomass-derived CDs exhibit low quantum yields and tend to emit in the blue-green light region, which hinders their application in biological systems [14]. Besides, most CDs have a surface rich in hydroxyl carboxyl groups, which imparts a negative effect and drives them to be retained more readily in lysosomes, making it difficult to target subcellular organelles such as mitochondria [15,16]. Leveraging the advantages of CDs to extend their emission wavelength for enhanced tissue penetration in bioimaging while precisely targeting mitochondria to interfere with ferroptosis and autophagy is one of the effective strategies for comprehensive treatment of AKI, which has not been reported.

Lophatherum-Gracile is a traditional Chinese medicine derived from food sources, employed for managing fever, urinary tract inflammation, and alleviating thirst [17,18]. It is rich in compounds such as rutin, quercetin, flavonoids, chlorogenic acid, vitexin, and porphyrin, which are responsible for its potential antioxidant properties [19]. The abundant phenolic hydroxyl groups allow it to chelate with Fe(II), positioning it as a potential inhibitor of Fe(II)-related diseases [20]. Widely distributed across China [21,22], Lophatherum-Gracile is an environmentally friendly and cost-effective renewable resource of carbon, priced at just $0.007 US dollars per gram. Additionally, its leaves are rich in chlorophyll to provide porphyrin groups. Zhang et al reported that the amino group of porphyrin binds to hydrogen ions to form protonated meso-tetrakis(4-aminophenyl) porphyrin (TAPP), which specifically targets the negatively charged membrane of mitochondria [23]. In a similar study, Handa et al. demonstrated that 2-oxogalaric acid carriers facilitate the accumulation of porphyrins within the inner mitochondrial membrane [24]. Taken together, these attributes make Lophatherum gracile, an excellent carbon source for CDs preparation, and investigating its potential in addressing ferroptosis-related inflammation and mitophagy holds considerable promise.

Inspired by CDs and their potential in treating acute AKI, we innovatively utilized the Lophatherum-Gracile as a carbon source. By combining boiling technology with hydrothermal methods, we develop high-safety and excellent-performance near-infrared L-CDs. As shown in Scheme 1 , L-CDs exhibit significant antioxidant properties, decreasing lipid peroxidation accumulation within cells, higher glutathione (GSH) levels, and inhibiting ferroptosis. L-CDs uptake by mitochondria, maintain ATP level in HK-2 cells, and regulate mitochondrial autophagic protein expression. Meanwhile, they passively target the liver and kidneys, maintaining the creatinine (CREA) and urea nitrogen (UREA) levels of the mouse kidney, so L-CDs can also treat and prevent AKI caused by chemotherapy drugs, with effects comparable to commercially available drugs (Fer-1 and DFO), while offering exceptionally high safety. This study lays the foundation for developing targeted and effective therapies for AKI. Of course, this effective treatment benefits from the excellent properties of L-CDs.

Here, Lophatherum gracile, a traditional Chinese medicine used for medicinal and edible purposes, was innovatively selected as a carbon source. It is rich in various compounds, such as rutin, quercetin, flavonoids, chlorogenic acid, vitexin, porphyrins, etc. (Fig. S1). The phenolic hydroxyl groups in these compounds not only have the ability to chelate divalent iron ions but also endow the material with potential antioxidant properties [25]. Porphyrin was prepared using traditional Chinese medicine decoction technology and solvothermal method, as shown in Fig. 1 a, and can be obtained by polymerization and carbonization in ethyl alcohol and 240 °C for 2 h. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Fig. 1c) revealed that L-CDs exhibit a dot-like morphology with an average diameter of approximately 2.5 nm (Fig. 1b) and are evenly dispersed. Atomic force microscopy (AFM, Fig. S3) measured the particle height to be about 3 nm, showing uniform size characteristics. Additionally, L-CDs possess well-defined lattice planes with an interplanar spacing of 0.22 nm, as revealed by high-resolution TEM (HRTEM, Fig. 1d), corresponding to the (100) plane of graphitic carbon [26]. X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis showed a sharp diffraction peak at 2θ ≈ 21° (Fig. 1e), which corresponds to the (002) diffraction peak of graphitic carbon [27], indicating that L-CDs contain sp2-hybridized carbon core with a graphitic structure. Raman spectroscopy provided additional evidence of both disordered (sp3) and graphitic (sp2) carbon, as indicated by the D and G bands at 1360 cm−1 and 1590 cm−1, respectively (Fig. 1f) [28]. The intensity ratio of the D band to the G band (ID/IG) was found to be 1.11, suggesting a significant presence of graphitic carbon domains in L-CDs. These findings confirm that L-CDs exhibit a degree of crystallinity, incorporating both graphitic and amorphous carbon structures. Additionally, the chemical composition of L-CDs was extensively examined. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy identified distinct peaks for OH /NH (3364 cm−1), CH (2920 cm−1), CN/CC/CO (1700 cm−1), CN (1458 cm−1), and CN (1180 cm−1) [28], indicating the presence of phenolic hydroxyl groups in L-CDs (Fig. 1g). The composition and chemical states of L-CDs were further analyzed in XPS spectroscopy, showing that they mainly consist of C 1s (83.97 %), O 1s (14.58 %), and N 1s (1.45 %) (Fig. 1h). The high-resolution XPS spectrum of C 1s was deconvoluted into three components: CC/CC (284.8 eV), CO/CN (286.3 eV), CN (287.0 eV)and CO (287.9 eV) (Fig. 1i) [28]. Additionally, in the O1s region, characteristic peaks of CO (532.5 eV) and CO (531.7 eV) [29,30] were also detected (Fig. 1j). The N1s spectrum was deconvoluted into four peaks at 398.7, 399.4, 400.0, and 401.6 eV, corresponding to pyridinic N, pyrrolic N, amino N, and graphitic N, respectively (Fig. 1k) [16,31]. These results suggested that porphyrin structures participate in the formation of heterocycles within L-CDs, and the residual chemical groups from Lophatherum gracile, particularly phenolic hydroxyl groups and porphyrin structures, remain on the surface of the L-CDs.

The optical properties of L-CDs are essential for their biomedical applications. In natural light, the aqueous solution of L-CDs appears colorless and transparent, but it emits a bright red fluorescence when exposed to 365 nm UV light (Fig. 2 a). The maximum emission wavelength is 405 nm, with a primary peak at 660 nm and a secondary peak at 730 nm (Fig. 2b), highlighting its near-infrared emission capabilities [8]. Additionally, the absolute quantum yield (QY) of L-CDs, measured precisely using an integrating sphere, is 13.36 % (Fig. S2), demonstrating its high luminescent efficiency. As the excitation wavelength is varied from 360 nm to 550 nm, the emission spectrum of L-CDs remains consistent (Fig. 2c), a feature indicative of excitation-independent emission. This suggests that the sample consists of a uniform fluorescent substance rather than a mixture of different materials. Related literature reported that chlorophyll decomposes at 160 °C and completely converts into a blue-emitting substance [28], while the preparation temperature of L-CDs in this study is 240 °C, significantly higher than the decomposition temperature of chlorophyll. Qin et al. demonstrated that chlorophyll, a key pigment in leaf photosynthesis, exhibits red fluorescence with a fluorescence lifetime of around 3–5 ns [32], whereas the fluorescence lifetime of L-CDs (11.79 ns) differs significantly (Fig. 3 d). Therefore, we speculated that the luminescence of L-CDs does not originate from unreacted free chlorophyll but rather from new luminescent centers formed during the production of CDs using Lophatherum-Gracile as the carbon source.

The photostability of L-CDs was further evaluated by monitoring fluorescence intensity across various pH levels, solution conditions, and time intervals. As shown in Fig. 2d–i, in various cationic (Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Ba2+, Cu2+, and NH4 +) and anionic environments (PO4 3−, Cl−, and CH3COO−), L-CDs exhibit excellent stability. In addition, L-CDs demonstrated excellent photostability in solutions with pH values spanning from 1 to 14, with negligible changes in fluorescence intensity under natural light exposure for 0–180 min, indicating good resistance to photobleaching.

Notably, although many CDs samples with high red emissions have been designed, however, only a few were made from eco-friendly biomass [11]. In general, L-CDs possess multiple advantages such as near-infrared emission, high quantum yield (QY), pH stability, stability in common biological ions, resistance to photobleaching, and biosafety, which demonstrates significant potential for applications in bioimaging and disease treatment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Response to Fe(II) and antioxidant properties of L-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The abundant hydroxyl groups on the surface of L-CDs inspire us to explore their antioxidant capacity and binding properties with Fe(II). When Fe(II) is present in L-CD’s solution, the fluorescence significantly decreases, indicating an interaction between them (Fig. 3a). Besides, the UV absorption peak at 405 nm exhibited a notable decline (Fig. 3b), while the electric potential increased (Fig. 3c). These findings provided compelling evidence for a significant interaction between the two components. The previous characterizations indicating a high density of phenolic hydroxyl groups on the surface of L-CDs, we hypothesize that a coordination reaction may have occurred, resulting in the observed alterations in fluorescence emission [33]. Therefore, we undertook a comprehensive investigation into the mechanisms underlying the fluorescence changes at the 666 nm emission peak. In general, the fluorescence quenching mechanisms of CDs can be classified into static quenching, dynamic quenching, fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), photoelectron transfer (PET), and internal filtering effects (IFE) [34]. The fluorescence lifetime of L-CDs was determined to be 11.79 ns (Fig. 3d). Following the addition of Fe(II), the fluorescence lifetime is slightly reduced to 11.76 ns (Fig. 3e). This minimal change in fluorescence lifetime suggested the potential presence of both IFE and static quenching phenomena between L-CDs and Fe(II) [35]. Additionally, within the wavelength range of 300 nm to 500 nm, the absorption peak of Fe(II) overlaps with the 405 nm absorption wavelength of L-CDs, suggesting that the internal filtering effect (IFE) may act as a quenching mechanism between L-CDs and Fe(II) (Fig. 3b). Moreover, based on the Stern-Volmer equation (Fig. 3f and Table S1), the Ksv values showed a significant decrease as the temperature increased from 25 °C to 35 °C. Furthermore, the Kq values for Fe(II) at all temperatures were significantly higher than the maximum Kq for collision quenching (1–2 × 10−10 L⋅mol−1⋅s−1) [36]. This further supports the conclusion that the fluorescence quenching of L-CDs by Fe(II) is primarily due to static quenching. Therefore, the quenching mechanisms in the L-CDs-Fe(II) system likely involve a combination of IFE and static quenching [37].

Further, DPPH analysis was used to evaluate the antioxidant capacity of L-CDs, and the performance differences were compared with commercial antioxidants (ascorbic acid) [38]. As the concentration of L-CDs increases, the characteristic absorption peak of DPPH at 517 nm progressively diminishes (Fig. 3i). This observation indicates that the free radical scavenging ability of L-CDs is enhanced with increasing concentration, comparable to that of ascorbic acid (Fig. S4). Remarkably, at a concentration of 80 μg/mL, the free radical scavenging rate achieves 90 %. (Fig. 3g). Compared to ascorbic acid, L-CDs show slightly lower efficacy at lower concentrations, but they become comparable at higher concentrations (Fig. 3h). The calculated EC50 value for L-CDs is 27.4805 μg/mL (Table S2), which is considered to reflect excellent antioxidant capability according to literature reports [39]. These findings suggested that L-CDs have the potential to serve as effective antioxidants. Moreover, the superior complexation ability of L-CDs with Fe(II) and their free radical scavenging capacity establish a foundation for their application in treating inflammation-related ferroptosis disorders.

The biocompatibility of L-CDs is one of the considerations in exploring their therapeutic feasibility. Human proximal tubular epithelial cells (HK-2) and human embryonic kidney (HEK) 293 T cells were exposed to different concentrations of L-CDs probes for 24 h. The findings showed that at concentrations as high as 400 μg/mL, the average cell viability remained at 96.6 % and 94.2 %, respectively. (Fig. 4 a and b), demonstrate the L-CDs exhibit good biocompatibility. To determine the intracellular uptake of L-CDs, we evaluated their uptake by HK-2 cells using flow cytometry (Fig. 4c–e). As the concentration of L-CDs treated with HK-2 cells increased from 0 to 100 μg/mL, the fluorescence intensity within the cells gradually increased. Over time, L-CDs (10 μg/mL) were progressively internalized into the cells, although a partial decrease in fluorescence intensity was observed at 8 h. These results suggested that L-CDs are highly biocompatible, easily internalized into the cells, and efficiently cleared, thus, highlighting their potential for use in cellular imaging and therapeutic applications.

Ferroptosis is a recently identified type of regulated cell death driven by oxidative damage and the activity of intracellular bioactive iron, which facilitates the formation of lipid peroxides, culminating in cell death [40]. The removal of Fe(II) or the addition of exogenous antioxidants can scavenge ROS and prevent ferroptosis. Erastin is also a commonly used inducer of ferroptosis [41]. It induces ferroptosis by inhibiting the Xcel system, responsible for the reverse transport of cysteine and glutamate, resulting in increased intracellular Fe(II) levels, decreased GSH synthesis, and elevated ROS levels [1]. Inspired by the chelation performance of L-CDs with Fe(II), we further evaluated their potential to inhibit ferroptosis. HK-2 cells were pre-incubated with Erastin to induce ferroptosis and subsequently treated with L-CDs. The results demonstrated that L-CDs could inhibit the decrease in GSH levels induced by Erastin (Fig. 4f), suppress lipid peroxidation (Fig. 4g), and scavenge ROS (Fig. 4h-i). The Fe(II)-sensitive fluorescent probe Ferro-Orange was employed to track intracellular Fe(II) levels in HK-2 cells, as shown in Fig. 4j. The presence of L-CDs reduced the intracellular Fe(II) content. Subsequently, we conducted a detailed analysis of the efficiency of different concentrations of L-CDs in reducing intracellular Fe(II) levels using flow cytometry. First, we measured the intracellular Fe(II) concentration at different time points following Erastin treatment using Ferro-Orange. The results indicated that the fluorescence intensity peaked after 8 h of incubation of HK-2 cells with Erastin (Fig. S5). Following this, we co-incubated different concentrations of L-CDs (0–25 μg/mL) with Erastin for 8 h, revealing that 10 μg/mL of L-CDs reduced intracellular Fe(II) levels from 12 % to 3.34 %, achieving an inhibition rate of 72.40 % (Fig. 4k–l). Therefore, we selected 10 μg/mL of L-CDs as the therapeutic concentration for subsequent experiments. Co-incubation of L-CDs (10 μg/mL) with varying concentrations of Erastin (5–20 μM) for 8 h (Fig. S6) demonstrated that L-CDs effectively mitigated ferroptosis induced by Erastin at all tested concentrations. Moreover, as the fluorescence intensity of the FerroOrange-Fe(II) probe increased, the fluorescence intensity of L-CDs decreased, indicating an interaction between L-CDs and intracellular Fe(II) to effectively ferroptosis (Fig. S7).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mitochondrial targeted imaging and inhibition of mitophagy
ce:section.ce:para: To further investigate the mechanism of action of L-CDs on cells, the imaging conditions for L-CDs were optimized. Initially, L-CDs function as a wash-free imaging agent (Fig. 5 a). Unlike commercial organelle dyes such as DAPI, cells treated with L-CDs do not require PBS washing to remove unabsorbed L-CDs from the culture medium while still achieving excellent imaging results, thus simplifying the imaging protocol. This observation could be due to the fluorescence quenching of L-CDs in PBS and the culture medium, which is only restored upon crossing the cell membrane into a less polar environment (Fig. S8). Next, the imaging conditions were optimized by co-culturing HK-2 cells with varying concentrations of L-CDs (1–100 μg/mL) for different durations (5–240 min) (Fig. S9). The results indicated that treating HK-2 cells with 10 μg/mL L-CDs for 30 min produced the best imaging outcomes.

The specific localization of L-CDs is crucial for their precise diagnosis and treatment. Confocal microscopy co-staining results demonstrated a high degree of overlap between the fluorescence signals of L-CDs and Mito-Tracker Green, while there is no overlap with lysosomes and nucleus [42] (Figs. 5b and S10). L-CDs, derived from bamboo leaves, contain negatively charged phenolic hydroxyl groups and positively charged porphyrin-related moieties. These results indicated that L-CDs accumulate in mitochondria after entering living cells.

To confirm the effect of L-CDs on mitochondria, Annexin V-FITC/ propidium iodide (PI) staining was performed to assess the effects of different treatments on HK-2 cells using flow cytometry. The results revealed that the proportions of apoptotic cells were 2.69 % in the control group (PBS + PBS), 19.4 % in the Erastin group (Erastin + PBS), and 12.0 % in the L-CDs group (Erastin + L-CDs) (Fig. 5c and d), respectively, indicating that L-CDs can moderately mitigate apoptosis. TEM images provide clearer and more direct observation of changes in mitochondrial morphology. The results are shown in Fig. 5e, where Erastin induces autophagy of mitochondria in HK-2 cells, leading to an increase in autophagy vesicles, while the other groups preserved normal structural morphology. This significant alteration in mitochondrial structure is a characteristic hallmark of mitochondrial autophagy, potentially linked to Erastin-induced excessive ROS that damages mitochondria, ultimately leading to mitophagy [43]. Therefore, we hypothesize that L-CDs concurrently inhibit mitophagy during the treatment of iron death-related inflammatory processes.

The PINK1/ Parkin signaling pathway is essential for the removal of damaged mitochondria and is activated during mitochondrial dysfunction [44]. Western blot analysis was used to evaluate the expression levels of mitochondrial Parkin and PINK1 in HK-2 cells treated with Control, Erastin, and L-CDs. As shown in Fig. 5f–g, the levels of PINK1 and Parkin proteins were significantly reduced in the L-CDs group compared to the Erastin group. Meanwhile, the PINK1/Parkin-mediated mitochondrial autophagy process involves various proteins, including LC3 and p62, in addition to Parkin and PINK1. LC3 protein is a critical factor in the formation of mitophagy, existing in two forms: LC3-I in the cytoplasm and LC3-II on the mitochondrial membrane. LC3-II is a specific marker molecule for mitophagy, and its protein level is positively correlated with the extent of mitophagy. LC3-II protein levels were measured across different groups using an ELISA kit, showing a notable decrease in LC3-II expression in the L-CDs group compared to the Erastin group. Furthermore, p62 protein, which serves as a marker for mitophagy, exhibited a negative correlation with mitophagy levels [45]. Fig. 5g illustrates that p62 protein expression is lower in the Control and L-CDs groups, while the p62 protein level in the Erastin group exceeds that of both the Control and L-CDs groups, further confirming that L-CDs inhibit Erastin-induced mitophagy. Mitochondria are the primary sites for ATP production within cells; hence, cellular ATP levels reflect mitochondrial status to some extent [46]. The detection kit showed that ATP levels in the L-CDs group were higher than those in the Erastin group (Fig. 5h). These findings suggest that L-CDs effectively inhibit Erastin-induced mitophagy, with the underlying inhibitory mechanisms detailed in Fig. 5i.

The biocompatibility of nanodrugs is crucial for their application in biomedical imaging and therapy. Despite numerous reports indicating that L-CDs possess excellent biocompatibility, we remain committed to enhancing their safety during the design phase. For instance, bamboo leaves, which have been used in traditional medicine and cuisine in China for thousands of years, are the precursor. By combining traditional Chinese herbal decoction methods with the solvothermal synthesis of L-CDs, ethanol was the reaction solvent without adding any additional reagents and subjected the precursor to high-temperature carbonization. A comprehensive evaluation of the biotoxicity of L-CDs was performed before in vivo application (Fig. 6 a). The results demonstrated that L-CDs at a concentration of 100 μg/mL exhibited minimal hemolytic activity, similar to that of the PBS control group (Fig. 6b). Healthy Babl/c mice were orally administered L-CDs (400 μg/mL, 100 μL) for seven days, with no significant change in body weight compared to the PBS group (Fig. 6c). A complete blood routine and biochemical analyses were performed on the mice after one and seven days of L-CDs administration (Fig. 6d and e), revealing no significant changes in serum biochemical markers compared to the PBS group, all within normal ranges. Furthermore, histopathological examination of key organs (heart, liver, spleen, lung, and kidney) from mice treated with PBS and L-CDs for seven days was conducted using hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) staining (Fig. 6f), which confirmed the absence of any significant morphological abnormalities, indicating good health status in the mice.

In vivo studies were conducted to investigate the biological distribution of L-CDs through gavage and tail vein injection (Fig. 6g), with hair removal and fasting for 12 h prior to imaging. As illustrated, no fluorescence was detected in the PBS control group at either visible light (666 nm) or near-infrared (NIR) wavelengths (730 nm) three hours post-gavage. Conversely, significant fluorescence was observed in the L-CDs group at the NIR wavelength (730 nm), with negligible fluorescence in the visible light range (666 nm). This further validates literature reports that longer wavelengths penetrate tissues more deeply [47], highlighting the advantages of NIR-emitting L-CDs for biomedical imaging. Notably, there was no difference in fluorescence between the gavage and tail vein injection groups. Given that the oral administration method aligns with traditional Chinese medicine practices, we ultimately selected gavage as the preferred delivery route.

Further, the analysis of L-CDs metabolism in the mice revealed that (as depicted in Fig. 6h), L-CDs predominantly accumulated in the liver and kidneys under both visible light (666 nm) and NIR (730 nm) wavelengths, indicating hepatic and renal metabolism, with complete clearance observed within 48 to 72 h, suggesting a favorable safety profile for L-CDs [8]. Collectively, these results indicated that L-CDs do not elicit any toxic reactions in vitro or in vivo, laying a solid foundation for their further use in biomedical therapy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluating the therapeutic efficacy in CDDP-induced AKI mice
ce:section.ce:para: Ferroptosis is a type of cell death driven by oxidative stress and ROS, closely linked to iron accumulation and lipid peroxidation [48]. Several studies have highlighted the critical role of ferroptosis in AKI induced by various factors. Regulating iron balance to suppress ferroptosis has become a promising strategy for treating AKI [49,50].

A CDDP-induced AKI mouse model was established to assess the potential of L-CDs probes as a treatment for AKI. (Fig. 7 a). Initially, we optimized the conditions for constructing the mouse model and assessed the levels of creatinine (CREA) and urea nitrogen (UREA) [51] in both normal and AKI mice, different doses of CDDP (20 mg/kg and 10 mg/kg) were administered at multiple time points (1 day and 5 days) to evaluate the effects (Fig. 7b and c). Following treatment with varying doses of CDDP, we observed a statistically significant increase in CREA levels, while UREA levels significantly rose only after five days of treatment (***P < 0.001). The elevation of UREA and CREA levels was attributed to renal dysfunction [41]. Furthermore, mice in the 10 mg/kg and 20 mg/kg treatment groups exhibited a continuous decrease in body weight over the five days (Fig. 7d and f), with a mortality rate of 75 % observed in the 20 mg/kg group (Fig. 7e and g). H&E staining of kidneys from different groups revealed significant tubular deformations (atrophy or dilation) and glomerular damage (fibrosis or vacuolation) in CDDP-induced mice [51,52], while the kidneys of normal mice showed negligible damage (Fig. 7i). TUNEL staining indicated the presence of apoptosis in the kidneys of all model mice. These experimental results confirmed that CDDP (20 mg/kg and 10 mg/kg) effectively induces ferroptosis-related renal dysfunction in BALB/c mice.

Subsequently, L-CDs as a preventive treatment for AKI mice and compared their efficacy with two ferroptosis inhibitors (Fer-1 and DFO) [51,53], with the CDDP group serving as a negative control and Control (PBS) as a positive control. According to previous reports, 12 h prior to CDDP administration (Fig. 7h), mice received intraperitoneal injections of PBS, Fer-1, DFO, and L-CDs at their respective effective doses. Blood samples were collected on the second-day post-CDDP treatment to assess UREA and CREA levels (Fig. 7k-l). The results demonstrated that L-CDs exhibited a favorable therapeutic effect in AKI mice, the rate of weight loss was significantly slower than that of the CDDP group (Fig. S11), comparable to those in the Control, Fer-1, and DFO groups, effectively inhibiting the increase of GPX4 protein levels (Fig. 7m). H&E and TUNEL staining results indicated that the kidneys of L-CDs-treated AKI mice appeared normal, with significantly reduced tubular and glomerular damage, as well as decreased apoptosis, akin to the outcomes observed in the Control, Fer-1, and DFO groups. Conversely, the CDDP group exhibited more pronounced damage and apoptosis (Fig. 7j). Similar therapeutic effects were noted in mice treated with (20 mg/kg, for 2 days) (Fig. S12). We further treated AKI mice five days post-model establishment (Figs. S13–S15), confirming that L-CDs not only prevent CDDP-induced ferroptosis nephritis but also demonstrate a certain therapeutic effect on established AKI. In summary, these results indicated that L-CDs effectively inhibit ferroptosis and delay or treat the progression of AKI.

This study presents the innovative design of biocompatible and highly safe renal-targeted L-CDs, derived from bamboo leaves. The porphyrins contained in Lophatherum-Gracile enhance their targeting ability towards mitochondria. Additionally, the surface of L-CDs is enriched with phenolic hydroxyl groups, enabling Fe(II) chelation and ROS clearance, thereby showcasing their multifaceted potential for treating AKI. This novel application suggests promising future applications of L-CDs in the treatment of diseases related to ferroptosis.

First, we established the safety of L-CDs in biological imaging and therapeutic contexts. Bamboo leaves have a longstanding history as traditional medicine, often utilized for treating various ailments. By combining conventional decoction methods with a bottom-up synthesis approach for carbon dots, we produced L-CDs that exhibit no cytotoxicity or hemolysis. These L-CDs did not significantly alter hematological or biochemical indices in mice and were completely metabolized within 2–3 days, laying a solid foundation for subsequent biological applications. Second, HK-2 cell experiments demonstrated that L-CDs effectively inhibit ferroptosis induced by Erastin, characterized by reductions in intracellular divalent iron levels, ROS clearance, and lipid peroxidation suppression. The therapeutic effect increased with higher concentrations of L-CDs, indicating their efficacy in treating ferroptosis. Furthermore, through cellular co-localization experiments, L-CDs successfully targeted mitochondria and inhibited mitophagy and cellular apoptosis while maintaining ATP levels, thus providing robust support for subsequent treatment in AKI mouse models. Finally, compared to commercial ferroptosis inhibitors, L-CDs demonstrated significant effectiveness in preventing the occurrence and progression of AKI, exhibiting strong inhibitory effects regardless of varying CDDP dosages or treatments initiated five days post-model establishment.

In summary, our designed near-infrared L-CDs possess the ability to target mitochondria, chelate Fe(Ⅱ), and clear ROS, enabling efficient treatment of ferroptosis-related inflammation and mitophagy. L-CDs are highly effective in a CDDP-induced AKI mouse model. This research not only provides a new perspective on non-toxic treatments for cellular ferroptosis but also proposes a novel method for the preparation of near-infrared carbon dots, paving the way for significant future research into diseases related to ferroptosis and mitophagy.
Detailed experimental materials and methods can be found in the Supporting Information. The animal experiments adhered to the guidelines of the Xiamen University Animal Care and Use Committee.
